---
title: "How to run, 'service network-manager restart' in this script?"
date: 2014-04-10
forum: General Help
---

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-10
After sleep, I have to run that to bring up networking again

Just typing it in does not work.
```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    su service network-manger restart 
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```

```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"
SCRIPT2="service network-manager restart"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT2" 
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-10
ok tried this did not work, how can you start a service in a script?
```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"
SCRIPT2="service network-manager restart"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    su "$SCRIPT2" 
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```

---

### Post by Xanthius on 2014-04-10
I'm not sure, but isn't the command just simply: "sudo restart network-manager"?

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2014-04-10
How are you running the script? restarting the network-manager service requires root, so you'll either need to run the whole script with su**do** (not su - the root account is disabled, at least in the default installation), or use sudo service network-manager restart. This will either require an interactive password or placing a NOPASSWD exception in the sudoers file.

Do you really need to restart the service though? what actual problem are you trying to address? If this is a user-script, you might want to look at what's possible with nmcli e.g.

```
nmcli nm enable false && nmcli nm enable true
```

since it doesn't require root privileges (at least under the default policykit rules).

---

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-10
Yes, I have to restart the service because my modification to udev rules for some reason leaves the network unconfigured when it awakens from sleep.

This scripts starts up xgps when it comes out of sleep. I want to add in a restart of the service also.

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-10
> **steeldriver said:**
> How are you running the script? restarting the network-manager service requires root, so you'll either need to run the whole script with su**do** (not su - the root account is disabled, at least in the default installation), or use sudo service network-manager restart. This will either require an interactive password or placing a NOPASSWD exception in the sudoers file.

Do you really need to restart the service though? what actual problem are you trying to address? If this is a user-script, you might want to look at what's possible with nmcli e.g.

```
nmcli nm enable false && nmcli nm enable true
```

since it doesn't require root privileges (at least under the default policykit rules).

I just tried that and does not work.
What works is

```
boat@boat-MS-7529:~$ sudo service network-manager restart
[sudo] password for boat: 
network-manager stop/waiting
network-manager start/running, process 10038
boat@boat-MS-7529:~$ 

```

---

### Post by Toz on 2014-04-10
If this file is in /etc/pm/sleep.d and is being executed on resume from sleep, then this should work:
```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    service network-manger restart 
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```
If it doesn't work, post back the contents of /var/log/pm-suspend.log for examination.

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2014-04-10
That link appears to be for restarting SYSV style services - it's not the recommended way of restarting upstart-based services such as network-manager

---

---

### Post by Toz on 2014-04-10
The /var/log/pm-suspend.log file might be more helpful in this instance.

The other thing to try is to remove the file in /etc/pm/sleep.d and try sleep again to see if networking comes back without it in effect - maybe its your other udev edits that are causing the network problem.

---

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-10
I am pretty sure my changing the udev rule file is the cause, although why i do not have a clue.

 su "network-manager restart"

```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    su "network-manager restart"
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```

### Post by steeldriver on 2014-04-10
```
su "network-manager restart"
```

means "start an interactive shell as a user called network-manager restart". It's asking you for "network-manager restart"'s login password. You probably just want the ***command***

```
service network-manager restart
```

(that's assuming the script is run  as root - if it's not you will need to prepend **sudo **(not su) and arrange for it to be run without a password.

### Post by Toz on 2014-04-10
Try this version of the script:
```
#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
    /usr/sbin/service network-manager restart
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```

Make sure the script is saved in /etc/pm/sleep.d and it is owned by root (you'll have to use sudo to put the script there).

with permission set to not be executable, networking comes up after awaken from sleep.

### Post by Toz on 2014-04-11
> **sdowney717 said:**
> yes, I just verified, with permission set to execute, turns off networking.
with permission set to not be executable, networking comes up after awaken from sleep.

Lets try changing the order of execution in that script file. Lets restart the network first, then fire up xgps:
```

#!/bin/bash

USERID=boat
SCRIPT="/usr/bin/xgps"

case "$1" in
  suspend|hibernate)
     ;;
  resume|thaw)
    /usr/sbin/service network-manager restart
    sleep 5
    export DISPLAY=":0"
    export XAUTHORITY="/home/$USERID/.Xauthority"
    su $USERID -c "$SCRIPT"
     ;;
  *) exit $NA
     ;;  
esac
```
I've added a "sleep 5" in there to give time for the network to restart. If it still doesn't work, trying upping the time. If it does work, try decreasing the time.

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-11
Well, Ubuntu unity and suspend are also now foo barred.

Totally screwed up. What is odd is it works for awhile, but it is fooling you. Eventually the script starts failing, cant suspend, cant reboot, pull the plug to restart and then every boot gives system error related to sleep. You cant really go past 10 suspends and then it starts acting up.
Seems to also get worse the more you try to suspend, the more likely it wont work and after awhile wont suspend at all.
That system error popup on a boot now takes 10 cancels to clear it off. 

I am thinking the OS is logging an error due to udev and or the suspend script, and when it hits some level, it explodes and dies.

This udev and sleep stuff is very sensitive and if you make odd changes it does not like it. I may have to do a total reinstall, or maybe could a new user give it a fresh start?

### Post by steeldriver on 2014-04-12
How much swap do you have? how much RAM?

Did the computer the original HDD came from have less RAM than the one it's now attached to (using the enclosure)?

```

swapon -s

lshw -C memory

```

---

### Post by sdowney717 on 2014-04-12
> **steeldriver said:**
> How much swap do you have? how much RAM?

Did the computer the original HDD came from have less RAM than the one it's now attached to (using the enclosure)?

```

swapon -s

lshw -C memory

```

lets see, 
the computer at boat has 4 gb ddr2
the computer at home where it is running right now has 8gb ddr2

so 4gb less memory
